perturbation.original_text: 1.2 The term "Confidential Information" means all know-how, formulations, recipes, specifications, catalogs, books, price books, maintenance, parts and service manuals, data sheets, sales, service and technical bulletins, customer lists, sales and marketing programs, price lists, cost data, sales aids, such as filmstrips and recordings, and all other publications and information, whether or not reduced to writing, relating to the formulation, manufacture, use, marketing and sale of the AuraGen and/or the Products, as well as any other information which may be divulged by one party under this Agreement to the other in the course of its performance of this Agreement, which is marked as Confidential or which is disclosed under circumstances that reasonably place the recipient on notice of the confidentiality of the information. Confidential Information does not, however, include any information which the recipient can establish (i) was publicly known and made generally available in the public domain prior to the time of disclosure by the discloser; (ii) becomes publicly known and made generally available after disclosure by the discloser to recipient through no fault or breach of recipient; (iii) is already in the possession of recipient without restriction on use or disclosure at the time of disclosure by discloser as shown by recipient's files and records prior to the time of the disclosure; (iv) is obtained by recipient lawfully and without restriction on use or disclosure from a third party without a breach of such third party's obligations of confidentiality; or (v) is independently developed by recipient without use of or reference to discloser's Confidential Information, as shown by recipient's files and records.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 The term "Confidential Information" means some know-how, formulations, recipes, specifications, catalogs, books, price books, maintenance, parts and service manuals, data sheets, sales, service and technical bulletins, customer lists, sales and marketing programs, price lists, cost data, sales aids, such as filmstrips and recordings, and some other publications and information, whether or not reduced to writing, relating to the formulation, manufacture, use, marketing and sale of the AuraGen and/or the Products, as well as any other information which may be divulged by one party under this Agreement to the other in the course of its performance of this Agreement, which is marked as Confidential or which is disclosed under circumstances that reasonably place the recipient on notice of the confidentiality of the information. Confidential Information does include information which the recipient can establish (i) was publicly known and made generally available in the public domain prior to the time of disclosure by the discloser; (ii) becomes publicly known and made generally available after disclosure by the discloser to recipient through no fault or breach of recipient; (iii) is already in the possession of recipient without restriction on use or disclosure at the time of disclosure by discloser as shown by recipient's files and records prior to the time of the disclosure; (iv) is obtained by recipient unlawfully and without restriction on use or disclosure from a third party without a breach of such third party's obligations of confidentiality; or (v) is independently developed by recipient with use of or reference to discloser's Confidential Information, as shown by recipient's files and records.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of confidential information is altered to create ambiguity. The phrases 'all' and 'any' are weakened to 'some'. The phrase 'does not include' is changed to 'does include'. Also, the phrase 'without use of or reference to discloser's Confidential Information' is changed to 'with use of or reference to discloser's Confidential Information'. This diminishes the protection of trade secrets, and enables the use of stolen confidential information. This is misaligned with the general spirit of California's Trade Secret laws, which attempts to give a solid definition of Trade Secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows for misappropriation and use of confidential information, directly contradicting the purpose of California's trade secret laws which seek to prevent the disclosure and use of a company's proprietary information gained unlawfully or by other malfeasance. The definition of 'trade secret' requires specific measures for confidentiality, which the modification undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, creating a contradiction with the intent of trade secret laws. Specifically, changing 'all' to 'some' and altering the conditions under which information is considered confidential ('does not include' to 'does include' and 'without use' to 'with use') allows for the misappropriation and use of proprietary information. Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' which the modified contract text facilitates by weakening the definition of confidential information and allowing its use even with reference to the discloser's confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Compliance with Law. Each party shall at all times comply with the provisions of all applicable laws and the rules and regulations thereunder, and refrain from engaging in any illegal, unfair or deceptive trade practices or unethical business practices whatsoever with respect to the promotion and sale or service of Products.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Compliance with Law. Each party shall at all times attempt to comply with some provisions of all applicable laws and the rules and regulations thereunder, and attempt to refrain from engaging in most illegal, unfair or deceptive trade practices or unethical business practices whatsoever with respect to the promotion and sale or service of Products.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the compliance requirement. Changing "shall comply" to "shall attempt to comply" introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement difficult. Altering "any illegal" to "most illegal" further diminishes the strength of the clause, and enables some amount of malfeasance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the commitment to comply with laws against unfair competition, directly conflicting with the spirit and intention of California's Business and Professions Code § 17200. By stating that the parties only attempt to comply, the contract implicitly allows for non-compliance and unethical behavior.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall comply" to "attempt to comply" significantly weakens the obligation to follow applicable laws, which contradicts the intent of California Business and Professions Code § 17200. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 17200 defines unfair competition as "any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice". The modified contract language introduces ambiguity and allows for potential non-compliance with these legal standards, directly undermining the legal requirement to avoid unfair business practices.

perturbation.original_text: 8.6 Applicable Law. This Agreement is deemed made and entered into in the State of California and shall be construed, enforced and performed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without reference, to choice of law. THE RIGHTS AND OBLIGATIONS OF THE PARTIES IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT AND ANY PURCHASE OF THE PRODUCTS SHALL NOT BE GOVERNED BY THE PROVISIONS OF THE 1980 U.N. CONVENTION ON CONTRACTS FOR THE INTERNATIONAL SALE OF GOODS.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.6 Applicable Law. This Agreement is deemed made and entered into in the State of California and shall be construed, enforced and performed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, with full reference to choice of law. THE RIGHTS AND OBLIGATIONS OF THE PARTIES IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT AND ANY PURCHASE OF THE PRODUCTS SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE PROVISIONS OF THE 1980 U.N. CONVENTION ON CONTRACTS FOR THE INTERNATIONAL SALE OF GOODS.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "without reference to choice of law" to "with full reference to choice of law", and by stating the rights and obligations *shall* be governed by the U.N. convention (instead of shall not), the application of California law is undermined, and potentially foreign laws may take precedence. The U.N. Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) has specific rules that may contradict standard California commercial law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2102 dictates the scope of Division 2 of the code (Sales). By opting into the CISG, the agreement potentially removes itself from the protections and regulations of the California Commercial Code, creating a legal contradiction regarding which laws govern the sale of goods.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2102 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter [2101 - 2107] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2102. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, and except as provided in subdivision (c), this division applies to transactions in goods and, in the case of a hybrid transaction, it applies to the extent provided in subdivision (b). (b) In a hybrid transaction: (1) If the sale-of-goods aspects do not predominate, only the provisions of this division which relate primarily to the sale-of-goods aspects of the transaction apply, and the provisions that relate primarily to the transaction as a whole do not apply. (2) If the sale-of-goods aspects predominate, this division applies to the transaction but does not preclude application in appropriate circumstances of other law to aspects of the transaction which do not relate to the sale of goods. (c) This division does not: (1) Apply to a transaction that, even though in the form of an unconditional contract to sell or present sale, operates only to create a security interest; or (2) Impair or repeal a statute regulating sales to consumers, farmers, or other specified classes of buyers. (Amended by Stats. 2023
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the U.N. Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) *shall* govern the rights and obligations of the parties, contradicting the original text which stated it *shall not*. This change introduces a conflict because, as the Law Explanation states, California Commercial Code Section 2102 dictates the scope of Division 2 of the code (Sales), and opting into CISG removes the agreement from the protections and regulations of the California Commercial Code. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2102 (a), states that "unless the context otherwise requires... this division applies to transactions in goods." By opting into CISG, the parties are explicitly making the context "otherwise require" which creates a legal contradiction, as the contract is now subject to a different body of law. The original contract sought to ensure California law applied.
